full_response:
Geodesic equation in arbitrary coordinates  

 U ᵅ ∇ₐ Uᵇ = 0  or, in component form  dUᵇ/dτ + Γᵇ_{ac} Uᵃ Uᶜ = 0.

Step 1:  Christoffel symbols in Cartesian Minkowski coordinates  
In inertial Cartesian coordinates (t,x,y,z) of flat space–time, the metric η_{μν}=diag(-1,1,1,1) is constant, so all Christoffel symbols vanish: Γᵇ_{ac}=0.

Hence along a geodesic in these coordinates we must have  

 dUᵇ/dτ = 0  (for every component b).

Step 2: 4-velocity of a uniformly accelerated observer  
For proper acceleration α the world-line is  

 t(τ)= (1/α) sinh(ατ), x(τ)= (1/α) cosh(ατ), y=z=0.

Differentiating with respect to proper time τ gives the 4-velocity  

 U^μ = (dt/dτ, dx/dτ, 0, 0)  
    = (cosh(ατ), sinh(ατ), 0, 0).

Step 3: Evaluate dU^μ/dτ  
Compute component-wise derivatives:

 dU^t/dτ = d/dτ[cosh(ατ)] = α sinh(ατ),  
 dU^x/dτ = d/dτ[sinh(ατ)] = α cosh(ατ),  
 dU^y/dτ = dU^z/dτ = 0.

Thus the 4-acceleration is  

 A^μ ≡ dU^μ/dτ = (α sinh(ατ), α cosh(ατ), 0, 0) ≠ 0.

Step 4: Compare with the geodesic condition  
For a geodesic we require dU^μ/dτ = 0, but the uniformly accelerated observer has dU^μ/dτ ≠ 0. Therefore the geodesic equation is not satisfied.

Conclusion: In Minkowski space, a uniformly accelerated observer experiences a non-zero proper 4-acceleration and hence does not follow a geodesic.

\boxed{\text{Uniformly accelerated observers in flat space do \emph{not} follow geodesics because }dU^\mu/d\tau\neq 0\text{ while }\Gamma^\mu_{\alpha\beta}=0,\text{ so the geodesic equation is violated.}}  

Confidence: 0.86
